stimulus: The city council plans to reduce traffic congestion by building a new highway bypass around the downtown area. Council members argue that commuters currently driving through downtown will use the bypass instead, thereby reducing the number of vehicles on downtown streets during peak hours.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the argument?
choice_a: At least some commuters who currently drive through downtown do so primarily because no convenient alternative route exists.
choice_b: The bypass will be completed within the next fiscal year.
choice_c: Building the bypass is the most cost-effective way to reduce downtown congestion.
choice_d: Traffic congestion in the downtown area has worsened over the past decade.
choice_e: The bypass will be able to accommodate all commuters who currently drive through downtown.
explanation: The argument assumes commuters drive through downtown because there is no alternative. Negating A: if no commuters drive through downtown due to lack of alternatives, a bypass would not divert them, and the argument collapses.
explanation_a: Correct. If commuters drive through downtown for reasons other than a lack of alternatives, they would not switch to the bypass, and the argument fails.
explanation_b: Wrong. The timeline for completion is irrelevant to whether the bypass will reduce congestion once built.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument claims the bypass will work, not that it is the most cost-effective solution.
explanation_d: Wrong. Whether congestion has worsened is background information irrelevant to whether the bypass will reduce current congestion.
explanation_e: Wrong. This is too strong. The argument only requires that some commuters use the bypass, not that it accommodate all of them.

stimulus: A pharmaceutical company recently discovered that a compound found in a certain deep-sea sponge can inhibit the growth of cancer cells in laboratory conditions. The company's researchers have therefore concluded that this compound could eventually be developed into an effective cancer treatment for humans.
stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
choice_a: the deep-sea sponge is the only organism that produces this compound
choice_b: no currently available cancer treatments are derived from marine organisms
choice_c: the compound will prove more effective than all existing cancer treatments
choice_d: the compound's cancer-inhibiting properties observed in the laboratory are not entirely lost when the compound is introduced into the human body
choice_e: the deep-sea sponge can be harvested in sufficient quantities to supply a global market
explanation: The argument moves from lab results to potential human treatment. Negating D: if the compound's properties are entirely lost in the human body, it could never become an effective human treatment, destroying the argument.
explanation_a: Wrong. Whether other organisms produce the compound is irrelevant to whether this compound can become a treatment.
explanation_b: Wrong. The existence of other marine-derived treatments has no bearing on whether this particular compound could work.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument claims the compound could be effective, not that it would be superior to all existing treatments. This is too strong.
explanation_d: Correct. If the compound loses all effectiveness in the human body, the leap from lab results to human treatment fails entirely.
explanation_e: Wrong. While supply is a practical concern, the argument is about whether the compound could be developed into a treatment, not about scalability.

stimulus: Professor Haynes argues that the university should require all students to take at least one philosophy course. She points out that philosophy courses develop critical thinking skills, and that students with strong critical thinking skills tend to perform better in their careers after graduation.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which Professor Haynes's argument relies?
choice_a: Philosophy is the only academic discipline that develops critical thinking skills.
choice_b: Most students do not currently choose to take philosophy courses voluntarily.
choice_c: Career performance is something the university should consider when designing its curriculum.
choice_d: Students who are required to take a course learn just as much as those who take it voluntarily.
choice_e: Critical thinking skills cannot be developed outside of a formal academic setting.
explanation: Haynes argues the university should require philosophy because it helps careers. Negating C: if career performance is not something the university should consider in curriculum design, then the career-performance benefit provides no reason for the requirement.
explanation_a: Wrong. The argument does not need philosophy to be the only discipline that builds critical thinking. It only needs philosophy to be effective at doing so.
explanation_b: Wrong. Whether students currently take philosophy voluntarily is irrelevant to whether it should be required for its benefits.
explanation_c: Correct. The argument uses career performance as a reason for a curricular requirement. If career performance is irrelevant to curriculum design, the justification evaporates.
explanation_d: Wrong. While this might strengthen the argument, the argument does not depend on equal learning outcomes between required and voluntary enrollment.
explanation_e: Wrong. This is too strong. The argument only needs philosophy courses to develop critical thinking, not that no other setting can.

stimulus: A new law requires that all commercial buildings install energy-efficient lighting within two years. Supporters of the law argue that it will significantly reduce the city's overall electricity consumption, since commercial buildings account for a large share of the city's total electricity use.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the argument?
choice_a: Residential buildings consume less electricity than commercial buildings do.
choice_b: Energy-efficient lighting is not already installed in most commercial buildings in the city.
choice_c: The cost of energy-efficient lighting has decreased substantially in recent years.
choice_d: The law will also encourage residential building owners to install energy-efficient lighting.
choice_e: No other city has enacted a similar requirement for commercial buildings.
explanation: The argument claims the law will reduce consumption. Negating B: if most commercial buildings already have energy-efficient lighting, the law would change very little, and the predicted significant reduction would not occur.
explanation_a: Wrong. The relative consumption of residential versus commercial buildings is not needed.
explanation_b: Correct. If energy-efficient lighting is already installed in most commercial buildings, the mandate produces no meaningful change.
explanation_c: Wrong. The cost of the lighting is irrelevant to whether switching to it will reduce consumption.
explanation_d: Wrong. The argument is specifically about commercial buildings. Residential spillover is outside its scope.
explanation_e: Wrong. Whether other cities have similar laws has no bearing on whether this law will reduce this city's electricity consumption.

stimulus: Archaeologists found pottery fragments at a site in southern France that closely resemble pottery known to have been produced in ancient Greece. The archaeologists concluded that there was trade between ancient Greece and the inhabitants of southern France during the period when the pottery was made.
stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
choice_a: the pottery fragments are the oldest artifacts found at the site in southern France
choice_b: ancient Greek pottery has not been found at any other sites in western Europe
choice_c: trade between ancient Greece and southern France has been confirmed by written historical records
choice_d: the pottery found in southern France was used for the same purposes as similar pottery in Greece
choice_e: the inhabitants of southern France did not independently develop techniques for producing pottery resembling Greek pottery
explanation: The argument infers trade from the resemblance of pottery. Negating E: if the inhabitants independently developed similar pottery, the resemblance does not indicate trade, and the conclusion is unsupported.
explanation_a: Wrong. Whether these are the oldest artifacts at the site is irrelevant to whether they indicate trade with Greece.
explanation_b: Wrong. Greek pottery at other sites would not undermine the claim that trade occurred at this site.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument draws its conclusion from physical evidence. It does not require written confirmation.
explanation_d: Wrong. The function of the pottery is irrelevant to whether its presence indicates trade.
explanation_e: Correct. If the local inhabitants independently made similar pottery, the resemblance proves nothing about trade, and the argument collapses.

stimulus: Monarch butterflies that migrate to central Mexico each winter navigate with remarkable precision, arriving at the same mountain forests their ancestors visited. Scientists believe that the butterflies use Earth's magnetic field to guide their journey. If this is correct, the butterflies must possess some biological mechanism for detecting magnetic fields.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the scientists' reasoning relies?
choice_a: Monarch butterflies are the only insect species capable of long-distance migration.
choice_b: The biological mechanism for detecting magnetic fields is unique to monarch butterflies.
choice_c: No other navigational cue, such as the position of the sun, could fully account for the butterflies' precise navigation.
choice_d: Earth's magnetic field in the region between the butterflies' starting point and central Mexico has not changed significantly over time.
choice_e: Monarch butterflies that do not migrate also possess the ability to detect magnetic fields.
explanation: The scientists believe magnetic fields guide the butterflies. Negating C: if another cue like the sun could fully account for the navigation, there would be no reason to invoke magnetic field detection, undermining the hypothesis.
explanation_a: Wrong. Whether other insects migrate is irrelevant to how monarchs navigate.
explanation_b: Wrong. The argument does not require that no other species shares this mechanism.
explanation_c: Correct. If another cue fully explains the navigation, the magnetic field hypothesis loses its basis.
explanation_d: Wrong. While plausible, the argument does not depend on the field being unchanged. Even a changed field could guide butterflies.
explanation_e: Wrong. Non-migrating monarchs are outside the scope of the argument about migratory navigation.

stimulus: The museum director argued that the museum should acquire the recently discovered painting attributed to Vermeer. She noted that if the painting is authentic, it would be worth many times the asking price and would dramatically increase visitor attendance. Therefore, she concluded, the purchase would be a wise investment.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the museum director's argument?
choice_a: The museum does not already own any paintings by Vermeer.
choice_b: The painting is in excellent condition and would not require extensive restoration.
choice_c: All major art museums should strive to own at least one Vermeer painting.
choice_d: There is a reasonable likelihood that the painting is authentic.
choice_e: The museum has already consulted independent art experts about the painting's authenticity.
explanation: The director's case rests on the painting being authentic. Negating D: if there is no reasonable likelihood it is authentic, the conditional benefits would not materialize, making the purchase unwise.
explanation_a: Wrong. Whether the museum already has a Vermeer is irrelevant to whether this purchase would be a wise investment.
explanation_b: Wrong. The argument focuses on value and attendance, not on physical condition.
explanation_c: Wrong. This is a general normative claim the argument does not require.
explanation_d: Correct. The benefits are contingent on authenticity. If there is no reasonable chance it is authentic, the purchase is unjustified.
explanation_e: Wrong. Whether experts have been consulted is a procedural detail unrelated to the investment logic.

stimulus: A tenants' rights organization argued that the proposed rent increase for the Maple Street apartment complex violates the city's rent stabilization ordinance. The ordinance caps annual rent increases at three percent for buildings constructed before 1980. The organization claims the landlord's proposed seven percent increase is therefore illegal and must be reversed by the housing board.
stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
choice_a: the landlord has not made significant capital improvements to the building that would qualify for an exemption under the ordinance
choice_b: other tenants in the building have also objected to the proposed rent increase
choice_c: the city's rent stabilization ordinance applies to the Maple Street apartment complex
choice_d: the housing board has the authority to impose fines on landlords who violate the ordinance
choice_e: the tenants currently pay below-market rent compared to similar apartments in the area
explanation: The argument assumes the ordinance applies to this specific building. If the Maple Street complex was built after 1980 or is otherwise exempt, the ordinance would not cap its rent increases, and the argument would collapse.
explanation_a: Incorrect. Capital improvement exemptions would weaken the argument but are not the foundational assumption it depends on.
explanation_b: Incorrect. Whether other tenants object is irrelevant to whether the increase violates the ordinance.
explanation_c: Correct. The entire argument rests on the ordinance applying to this building. Negating this assumption — if the building is not covered — destroys the conclusion.
explanation_d: Incorrect. The board's power to impose fines is about enforcement, not whether the increase is illegal.
explanation_e: Incorrect. The current rent level relative to market rates has no bearing on whether the increase violates the ordinance.

stimulus: Studies show that children who read for at least thirty minutes a day score higher on vocabulary tests than children who do not. A school board member therefore argues that mandating thirty minutes of daily reading time in all elementary schools will improve students' vocabulary scores across the district.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the school board member's argument relies?
choice_a: The correlation between reading time and vocabulary scores is not solely due to the fact that children with larger vocabularies tend to read more.
choice_b: Thirty minutes is the minimum amount of daily reading needed to improve vocabulary scores.
choice_c: Vocabulary scores are the most important measure of academic achievement in elementary schools.
choice_d: No elementary school in the district currently requires daily reading time.
choice_e: Children who read for more than thirty minutes a day score even higher on vocabulary tests.
explanation: The argument infers that mandating reading will improve vocabulary. Negating A: if the correlation exists only because children with large vocabularies choose to read more, mandating reading would not improve vocabulary.
explanation_a: Correct. If the causal direction is reversed, mandating reading will not cause vocabulary improvement, and the argument collapses.
explanation_b: Wrong. The argument does not claim thirty minutes is a minimum threshold, only that it is associated with higher scores.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument concerns vocabulary improvement specifically. It does not assume vocabulary is the most important measure.
explanation_d: Wrong. Even if some schools already require reading, the argument could still apply to those that do not.
explanation_e: Wrong. Whether more reading produces even higher scores is irrelevant to the core claim about thirty minutes.

stimulus: Ecologists have observed that a certain forest's bird population has declined sharply since a new pesticide was introduced in surrounding farmland. They conclude that the pesticide is responsible for the decline, noting that the pesticide is known to be toxic to insects that the birds rely on for food.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the ecologists' argument?
choice_a: The pesticide has not been banned in any other region.
choice_b: No other significant change in the birds' environment occurred during the same period.
choice_c: The birds in the forest do not eat any food other than insects.
choice_d: The pesticide is the most toxic chemical ever used in the surrounding farmland.
choice_e: Bird populations in other forests where the pesticide was not introduced have also declined.
explanation: The ecologists attribute the decline to the pesticide. Negating B: if another significant environmental change occurred, that change could explain the decline instead.
explanation_a: Wrong. The legal status of the pesticide elsewhere is irrelevant to whether it caused the decline here.
explanation_b: Correct. If another major change occurred simultaneously, the pesticide may not be the cause.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument only requires insects to be an important food source, not the only one. Too strong.
explanation_d: Wrong. The argument does not require the pesticide to be the most toxic ever used.
explanation_e: Wrong. If bird populations declined elsewhere without the pesticide, that would weaken, not support, the argument.

stimulus: A literary critic argues that the novel "Winds of Autumn" was influenced by the earlier work "Silent Meridian," pointing out that both novels feature a protagonist who abandons a promising career to pursue an unconventional life in a remote village. The critic concludes that the author of "Winds of Autumn" must have read "Silent Meridian" before writing her novel.
stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
choice_a: "Silent Meridian" was widely read and well-known at the time "Winds of Autumn" was written
choice_b: the author of "Winds of Autumn" has publicly acknowledged admiring "Silent Meridian"
choice_c: "Winds of Autumn" is considered the superior novel by most literary critics
choice_d: the similarities between the two novels are not purely coincidental
choice_e: no other novel published before "Silent Meridian" contains a similar plot element
explanation: The critic infers influence from similarity. Negating D: if the similarities are purely coincidental, there is no basis for concluding that one author read the other's work.
explanation_a: Wrong. The earlier novel need not have been widely known. The author could have encountered it in obscurity.
explanation_b: Wrong. The argument draws its conclusion from textual evidence, not from public statements.
explanation_c: Wrong. Which novel is considered superior is irrelevant to whether one influenced the other.
explanation_d: Correct. If the similarities are mere coincidence, the inference of influence is baseless.
explanation_e: Wrong. Even if earlier novels share the plot element, this specific work could still have influenced the author.

stimulus: The county legislature is considering a law that would prohibit stores from providing single-use plastic bags. Proponents argue that the ban will substantially reduce plastic waste in the county's landfills, since single-use plastic bags constitute a notable portion of such waste.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the proponents' argument relies?
choice_a: Reusable bags are preferred by a majority of the county's residents.
choice_b: Single-use plastic bags are the largest source of waste in the county's landfills.
choice_c: Consumers will not respond to the ban by purchasing plastic bags from stores in neighboring counties.
choice_d: The county legislature has the legal authority to enact such a ban.
choice_e: Stores will not simply replace plastic bags with paper bags that also end up in landfills.
explanation: The argument claims the ban will reduce plastic waste. Negating C: if consumers buy plastic bags from neighboring counties, the same bags enter the waste stream, and the ban fails to reduce landfill plastic waste.
explanation_a: Wrong. Consumer preference for reusable bags is not required. The ban applies regardless.
explanation_b: Wrong. The argument says bags are a notable portion, not the largest source. This is stronger than assumed.
explanation_c: Correct. If consumers circumvent the ban by getting bags elsewhere, plastic waste is not substantially reduced.
explanation_d: Wrong. Legal authority is a practical matter, not a logical assumption needed for the reasoning to hold.
explanation_e: Wrong. Paper bags are not plastic waste. Even if paper bag use increases, the argument about reducing plastic waste could still hold.

stimulus: Geologists have found that a particular rock formation in northern Canada contains mineral deposits typically formed only under extremely high pressure deep within Earth's crust. They therefore concluded that this rock formation was once located far below the surface and was later pushed upward by tectonic forces.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the geologists' argument?
choice_a: No surface-level geological process can produce the same mineral deposits that are typically formed under high pressure deep within Earth's crust.
choice_b: The rock formation is the oldest geological feature in northern Canada.
choice_c: Tectonic forces are still actively pushing rock formations upward in northern Canada.
choice_d: Similar mineral deposits have been found in rock formations on every continent.
choice_e: The geologists used the most advanced equipment available to analyze the rock formation.
explanation: The geologists infer deep origin from mineral composition. Negating A: if surface processes can produce these minerals, the minerals do not prove the rock was once deep underground.
explanation_a: Correct. If surface processes could create these minerals, their presence would not demonstrate deep origin.
explanation_b: Wrong. The age of the formation relative to other features is irrelevant.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument concerns past tectonic activity, not whether such forces are still active.
explanation_d: Wrong. The distribution of similar deposits globally has no bearing on this particular formation's origin.
explanation_e: Wrong. Equipment quality is irrelevant to the logical structure of the argument.

stimulus: A judge ruled that the defendant's confession was inadmissible because it was obtained during an interrogation in which the defendant was not informed of the right to an attorney. The prosecution argues that the confession should still be admitted because the defendant had previously been informed of this right during an earlier unrelated arrest.
stem: The prosecution's argument depends on the assumption that
choice_a: the right to an attorney applies only during formal interrogations, not during arrests
choice_b: being informed of the right to an attorney on a prior occasion satisfies the legal requirement to be informed before each interrogation
choice_c: the defendant's confession was truthful and corroborated by other evidence
choice_d: the defendant had an attorney available at the time of the interrogation
choice_e: most defendants are informed of their rights during every interrogation
explanation: The prosecution argues prior notification suffices. Negating B: if prior notification does not satisfy the requirement, the confession remains inadmissible, and the argument fails.
explanation_a: Wrong. The argument does not claim the right applies only in certain settings.
explanation_b: Correct. The prosecution's entire case rests on prior notification being legally sufficient.
explanation_c: Wrong. The issue is admissibility based on procedural rights, not the truth of the confession.
explanation_d: Wrong. The argument concerns notification of the right, not actual availability of an attorney.
explanation_e: Wrong. What typically happens with other defendants is irrelevant.

stimulus: A sociologist observes that neighborhoods with more community gardens tend to have lower crime rates. She concludes that community gardens help reduce crime by fostering a sense of shared responsibility and social cohesion among residents.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the sociologist's argument relies?
choice_a: Community gardens are the most effective way to reduce crime in urban neighborhoods.
choice_b: Neighborhoods without community gardens have no sense of social cohesion.
choice_c: Every neighborhood that has established a community garden has experienced a decline in crime.
choice_d: Community gardens have been shown to reduce crime in controlled scientific experiments.
choice_e: The lower crime rates in these neighborhoods are not entirely explained by other factors, such as higher income levels, that also correlate with the presence of community gardens.
explanation: The sociologist claims gardens reduce crime via social cohesion. Negating E: if confounding factors like income fully explain the correlation, the gardens are not the cause.
explanation_a: Wrong. The argument claims gardens help reduce crime, not that they are the most effective method. Too strong.
explanation_b: Wrong. The argument only claims gardens foster cohesion, not that cohesion is absent without them. Too extreme.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument makes a general claim, not a universal one.
explanation_d: Wrong. The argument is based on observational data. It does not assume controlled experiments have been conducted.
explanation_e: Correct. If confounding factors fully explain the correlation, the gardens play no causal role.

stimulus: Art historian Daniels contends that a recently discovered unsigned painting from the seventeenth century was created by Rembrandt. She bases this claim on the fact that the painting's use of light and shadow closely matches techniques found in Rembrandt's authenticated works from the same period.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by Daniels's argument?
choice_a: Rembrandt is the greatest painter of the seventeenth century.
choice_b: The painting was found in the same city where Rembrandt lived and worked.
choice_c: Modern technology can definitively determine the age of a painting.
choice_d: Rembrandt never signed any of his paintings.
choice_e: No other seventeenth-century painter employed light and shadow techniques closely matching those of Rembrandt.
explanation: Daniels attributes the painting to Rembrandt based on technique. Negating C: if other painters used closely matching techniques, the style alone does not identify Rembrandt as the creator.
explanation_a: Wrong. Rembrandt's relative ranking is irrelevant to whether this painting is his work.
explanation_b: Wrong. Provenance is not part of Daniels's stated reasoning, which relies solely on technique.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument is based on stylistic analysis, not technological dating.
explanation_d: Wrong. The argument notes this painting is unsigned. It does not assume Rembrandt never signed paintings generally.
explanation_e: Correct. If other painters closely matched Rembrandt's techniques, the stylistic evidence does not uniquely point to Rembrandt.

stimulus: A fitness trainer claims that her new high-intensity interval training program is effective for weight loss, citing the fact that her clients who followed the program for three months lost an average of fifteen pounds. She recommends the program to anyone seeking to lose weight.
stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
choice_a: high-intensity interval training is the only exercise method that produces weight loss
choice_b: all of the trainer's clients who followed the program wanted to lose weight
choice_c: fifteen pounds of weight loss in three months is considered medically significant
choice_d: the weight loss experienced by the trainer's clients was not primarily caused by dietary changes they made independent of the training program
choice_e: the trainer has formal certification in exercise science
explanation: The trainer credits her program for the weight loss. Negating D: if the clients' weight loss was primarily caused by independent dietary changes, the program itself was not the effective factor.
explanation_a: Wrong. The argument claims this program works, not that it is the only effective method. Far too strong.
explanation_b: Wrong. The clients' personal motivations are irrelevant to whether the program caused weight loss.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument claims the program is effective. Whether fifteen pounds is medically significant is a separate question.
explanation_d: Correct. If independent dietary changes caused the weight loss, the training program does not deserve credit.
explanation_e: Wrong. The trainer's credentials are irrelevant to the argument about the program's effectiveness.

stimulus: Under the proposed regulation, financial advisors would be required to disclose all conflicts of interest to their clients before providing investment recommendations. Advocates argue this will protect consumers from biased advice, since clients who know about conflicts can evaluate recommendations more critically.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the advocates' argument relies?
choice_a: At least some clients, upon learning of a conflict of interest, will take that information into account when evaluating recommendations.
choice_b: Most financial advisors currently have conflicts of interest that they do not disclose.
choice_c: Financial advisors currently have no obligation to act in their clients' best interests.
choice_d: The regulation will be difficult for regulatory agencies to enforce consistently.
choice_e: Conflicts of interest always lead financial advisors to give poor recommendations.
explanation: The argument claims disclosure enables critical evaluation. Negating C: if no clients would use the information, disclosure would not lead to more critical evaluation.
explanation_a: Correct. If clients uniformly ignore conflict-of-interest disclosures, the argued benefit disappears.
explanation_b: Wrong. While this would make the regulation more impactful, the logic does not require that most advisors currently hide conflicts.
explanation_c: Wrong. Current obligations are not relevant to whether disclosure will help consumers.
explanation_d: Wrong. Enforcement difficulty is a practical concern, not a logical assumption.
explanation_e: Wrong. The argument assumes conflicts can bias advice, not that they always lead to poor recommendations. Too strong.

stimulus: A political scientist notes that voter turnout in local elections is significantly lower than in national elections. She argues that implementing same-day voter registration for local elections would substantially increase turnout, since many eligible citizens do not vote in local elections because they missed the registration deadline.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the political scientist's argument?
choice_a: Same-day voter registration has never been tried in any jurisdiction.
choice_b: National elections already allow same-day voter registration in most jurisdictions.
choice_c: Low voter turnout in local elections is a greater problem than low turnout in national elections.
choice_d: Same-day registration would not increase the risk of voter fraud.
choice_e: The citizens who missed the registration deadline would actually go to the polls if they could register on election day.
explanation: The argument claims same-day registration would increase turnout among those who missed the deadline. Negating E: if those citizens would not actually vote even with same-day registration, turnout would not increase.
explanation_a: Wrong. Whether same-day registration has been tried elsewhere is irrelevant.
explanation_b: Wrong. The national election registration system is not relevant to the argument about local turnout.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument makes no comparative judgment about which level of turnout is more problematic.
explanation_d: Wrong. The argument is about increasing turnout, not about fraud.
explanation_e: Correct. If people who missed the deadline still would not show up, removing the barrier accomplishes nothing.

stimulus: A music professor argues that listening to classical music improves spatial reasoning abilities. As evidence, she cites a study in which participants who listened to a Mozart sonata before taking a spatial reasoning test scored higher than participants who sat in silence. She concludes that incorporating classical music into school curricula would boost students' spatial reasoning.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the music professor's argument relies?
choice_a: Mozart composed more sonatas than any other classical composer.
choice_b: The improvement in spatial reasoning observed in the study was not a temporary effect that would fade before students could benefit from it in school settings.
choice_c: Spatial reasoning is more important than other cognitive abilities for academic success.
choice_d: The participants in the study had no prior experience with classical music.
choice_e: Classical music is the only type of music that can improve spatial reasoning.
explanation: The professor generalizes from a one-time study to ongoing school benefit. Negating B: if the improvement is purely temporary and fades before it can be useful, incorporating classical music would not boost students' spatial reasoning.
explanation_a: Wrong. The number of Mozart's sonatas is irrelevant.
explanation_b: Correct. If the effect is too transient to benefit students in practice, the policy recommendation is unsupported.
explanation_c: Wrong. The argument does not compare spatial reasoning's importance to other abilities.
explanation_d: Wrong. Whether participants had prior experience does not affect the argument's logic.
explanation_e: Wrong. The argument recommends classical music specifically; it need not assume it is the only type that works.
